content.text: in Python's asyncio is a low-level object that represents the eventual result of an asynchronous operation — something that hasn't completed yet but will at some point in the future.

Think of it as a placeholder box. The box exists right now, you can pass it around and attach callbacks to it, but the actual value inside won't arrive until some asynchronous work finishes. When the work completes, the box is "filled in," and anything waiting on it wakes up.
is a class in Python's standard library that acts as a
for a result that will be set later. It is the foundational building block on top of which
and most of asyncio's higher-level machinery is built. Unlike
is not tied to a coroutine — it's just a container that can be manually resolved with a result, an exception, or a cancellation.

import asyncio

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
future = loop.create_future()
inside a coroutine, and that coroutine will suspend until the
wraps a coroutine and drives it automatically. But understanding
Many low-level asyncio APIs (protocol callbacks, custom event loop integrations, bridging between threads and the event loop) use raw
When integrating synchronous or threaded code with asyncio,
is often the glue that ties the two worlds together.
maintains internal state and a list of callbacks.
States a Future can be in:
— no result yet; the future has been created but not resolved.
— the future was cancelled before it was resolved.
— the future was resolved with either a result or an exception.
in a coroutine, the event loop suspends that coroutine and registers a callback on the
), the event loop invokes all registered callbacks, which causes any suspended coroutines to be scheduled for resumption.

# Conceptually, what `await future` does under the hood:
# 1. Checks if future is already done → if yes, returns result immediately
# 2. If not done, suspends the current coroutine
# 3. Registers a callback: "when this future finishes, re-schedule my coroutine"
# 4. Returns control back to the event loop

import asyncio

async def main():
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    future = loop.create_future()

    # Check state
    print(future.done())      # False — still pending
    print(future.cancelled()) # False

    # Set the result from somewhere (usually from a callback or another coroutine)
    future.set_result(42)

    # Now await it
    result = await future
    print(result)  # 42

    print(future.done())      # True
    print(future.result())    # 42 — safe to call synchronously now

asyncio.run(main())
— marks the Future as done with a value. Any coroutines awaiting it will be resumed. Raises
— marks the Future as done with an exception. Awaiting it will raise that exception.
if cancellation was successful (i.e., the future was still pending).
— returns the result if done, raises the stored exception if the Future was resolved with one, or raises
if result is normal). Same rules about state.
— registers a callable to be called when the Future finishes. The callable receives the Future as its only argument.
Future vs Task — the critical distinction
This is the most important relationship to understand when working with asyncio awaitables.
has no coroutine attached. Someone external must call
on it. It's a pure synchronous-to-async bridge or a manual signal.
wraps a coroutine and drives it step by step on the event loop. When the coroutine finishes, the
with the coroutine's return value, or

import asyncio

async def my_coro():
    await asyncio.sleep(1)
    return "done"

async def main():
    # Task: coroutine is wrapped and driven automatically
    task = asyncio.create_task(my_coro())
    result = await task
    print(result)  # "done"

    # Raw Future: you must resolve it manually
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    future = loop.create_future()
    loop.call_later(1, future.set_result, "manually set")
    result2 = await future
    print(result2)  # "manually set"

asyncio.run(main())
is what you use for concurrent coroutines. Raw
Writing low-level protocol or transport code.
Bridging between threads and the event loop.
Implementing custom awaitables or synchronisation primitives.
Working with callback-based APIs that need to signal a coroutine when done.
is tied to an event loop and is not thread-safe. It must be awaited inside async code.
. It is thread-safe and blocking — calling
on it blocks the calling thread.
You can bridge between them using

import asyncio
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

def blocking_io():
    import time
    time.sleep(2)
    return "thread result"

async def main():
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    executor = ThreadPoolExecutor()
    
    # Submit blocking work to a thread pool
    concurrent_future = executor.submit(blocking_io)
    
    # Wrap into an asyncio.Future so we can await it
    asyncio_future = asyncio.wrap_future(concurrent_future, loop=loop)
    
    result = await asyncio_future
    print(result)  # "thread result"

asyncio.run(main())
This is the standard pattern for running CPU-bound or blocking I/O work off the event loop thread without blocking it.
works with a Future — under the hood
, this is implemented as a generator that:
is already done. If so, returns the result immediately without suspending.
If not done, registers the current coroutine's resumption as a callback on the
s control back to the event loop.
is resolved, the event loop calls the callback, which schedules the coroutine's resumption.
returns the result (or raises the stored exception).
in a way that cooperates with the event loop's scheduling machinery.

# You can inspect this yourself:
import asyncio

async def demo():
    future = asyncio.get_event_loop().create_future()
    future.set_result(99)
    
    # The __await__ protocol
    gen = future.__await__()
    try:
        next(gen)  # Already done, so this raises StopIteration(99)
    except StopIteration as e:
        print(e.value)  # 99

asyncio.run(demo())
One of the most practical features of a
is the ability to register callbacks that fire when it completes, regardless of whether it finished with a result, an exception, or cancellation.

import asyncio

def on_done(future):
    if future.cancelled():
        print("Future was cancelled")
    elif future.exception():
        print(f"Future raised: {future.exception()}")
    else:
        print(f"Future result: {future.result()}")

async def main():
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    future = loop.create_future()
    future.add_done_callback(on_done)
    
    future.set_result("hello")
    # Callbacks are scheduled, not called synchronously
    # They run in the next iteration of the event loop

asyncio.run(main())
# Output: Future result: hello
callbacks are scheduled to run on the event loop — they don't run synchronously at the moment of
. This means they run after the current coroutine yields back to the event loop.
Practical use case — bridging callbacks to async code
A very common real-world use of raw
is converting callback-based APIs into awaitable ones. Suppose an older library uses callbacks:

import asyncio

# Simulate a legacy callback-based API
def legacy_fetch(url, on_success, on_error):
    import threading
    def _work():
        import time
        time.sleep(0.5)  # simulate network
        if "bad" in url:
            on_error(ValueError("bad URL"))
        else:
            on_success(f"data from {url}")
    threading.Thread(target=_work, daemon=True).start()

# Wrap it into an awaitable using Future
async def fetch_async(url):
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    future = loop.create_future()
    
    def on_success(result):
        # Must use call_soon_threadsafe because the callback fires from another thread
        loop.call_soon_threadsafe(future.set_result, result)
    
    def on_error(exc):
        loop.call_soon_threadsafe(future.set_exception, exc)
    
    legacy_fetch(url, on_success, on_error)
    return await future

async def main():
    result = await fetch_async("https://example.com/data")
    print(result)  # "data from https://example.com/data"

asyncio.run(main())
is critical here. Setting a result on an
from a thread other than the event loop thread without using
is unsafe and can cause race conditions or silent failures.
. Any coroutine awaiting it will have

import asyncio

async def waiter(fut):
    try:
        result = await fut
        print(f"Got: {result}")
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        print("Future was cancelled — handling gracefully")
        raise  # re-raise is important; don't swallow CancelledError

async def main():
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    future = loop.create_future()
    
    task = asyncio.create_task(waiter(future))
    
    await asyncio.sleep(0)  # let waiter start
    future.cancel()
    
    try:
        await task
    except asyncio.CancelledError:
        pass

asyncio.run(main())
# Output: Future was cancelled — handling gracefully
before trying to set a result in callback code.
Setting result from the wrong thread.
directly from a non-event-loop thread is a race condition. Always use
for cleanup, always re-raise it afterwards. Swallowing it breaks asyncio's cancellation propagation chain.
is a non-blocking synchronous accessor — it only works after the Future is done.
They have similar APIs but different semantics.
inside non-async code can bind the Future to a stale or wrong loop. Create Futures inside coroutines where the loop context is clear, or pass the loop explicitly.
created but never awaited and never resolved is a resource leak. Pending Futures hold references to their callbacks and can prevent garbage collection.
Always create Futures inside a running event loop context (inside a coroutine or an event loop callback), not at module level or in synchronous setup code.
to set results or exceptions on a Future — never call
for concurrent coroutines over manually managing raw Futures. Use raw
only when you genuinely need manual resolution — protocol integration, callback bridging, or custom synchronisation primitives.
gracefully in code that awaits Futures, and always re-raise it so cancellation propagates correctly up the call stack.
When writing done callbacks, do not assume they run synchronously. They are scheduled on the event loop and run in a future iteration, not at the exact moment
objects rather than rolling custom bridging code.
one-time channel with a waiting room
Imagine a locked box with a slot. When you
a Future, your coroutine enters the waiting room for that box and falls asleep. When someone (a callback, a thread, a protocol implementation) calls
, they drop the value into the box's slot, it unlocks, and the waiting room wakes everyone up. The box can only be filled once — any subsequent attempt to fill it raises
is just a special box that comes with a machine (the coroutine runner) bolted to the side, which automatically fills itself when the coroutine finishes.
is a low-level, awaitable container for a value that will arrive asynchronously — it is not tied to any coroutine.
that wraps a coroutine and drives it to completion automatically.
Awaiting a pending Future suspends the coroutine and registers it to be resumed when the Future resolves.
Raw Futures are primarily used for bridging callback-based or threaded code into the asyncio world.
when setting a Future's result from a non-event-loop thread.
are different types with similar APIs — use
Done callbacks are scheduled, not synchronous — they run on the next event loop iteration after the Future is resolved.
